title: Today's Tech Brief: UK banks offered access to OpenAI’s GPT-5.5
summary: UK banks get access to OpenAI's GPT-5.5, while Anthropic's Glasswing expansion leaves them out.
imageAlt: Image from source article for UK banks offered access to OpenAI’s GPT-5.5 amid exclusion from Anthropic’s Glasswing expansion
imageCaption: UK banks offered access to OpenAI’s GPT-5.5 amid exclusion from Anthropic’s Glasswing expansion
imageSourceTitle: UK banks offered access to OpenAI’s GPT-5.5 amid exclusion from Anthropic’s Glasswing expansion

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to Today's Tech Brief. I've got some interesting tech news to share with you, particularly in the world of AI and banking. It's always exciting to see how technology is shaping industries, and today we've got a couple of developments that caught my attention.

First up, UK banks have been offered access to OpenAI's GPT-5.5. For those who might not know, GPT-5.5 is a highly advanced language model capable of processing and generating human-like text. This could be a game-changer for banks, enabling them to automate customer service, improve data analysis, and more. Just imagine being able to get instant answers to your banking queries, or having your financial data analyzed in a flash.

However, it seems UK banks are not part of Anthropic's Glasswing expansion. Glasswing is a cybersecurity initiative aimed at protecting organizations from cyber threats. Anthropic has inducted 150 new organizations into the program, but notably, UK banks are not among them. This exclusion is quite interesting, especially given the current cybersecurity landscape. It's like they're being left out of a big cybersecurity party.

UK banks offered access to OpenAI’s GPT-5.5 amid exclusion from Anthropic’s Glasswing expansion is also worth a quick mention. The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
So that is the brief version: a few headlines, a little context, and just enough connective tissue to make the day easier to follow without turning the whole thing into homework.
